evidence: Domain row contracts in src/domains/generation/types/generationViewRow.ts and src/domains/generation/types/shotViewRow.ts merge persisted entities with UI-derived and optimistic fields into single exported types (`GenerationRow`, `Shot`), which blurs ownership boundaries between persistence/query/UI layers.

Overlay state orchestration is spread across multiple shared/UI components instead of a single clear boundary: src/shared/components/ModalContainer.tsx owns open-change gating, while src/shared/components/MediaTypeFilter.tsx and src/shared/components/MediaGalleryItem/components/InfoTooltip.tsx each re-wrap optional open/onOpenChange via overlay helpers, and pages/components like src/pages/Home/components/InstallInstructionsModal.tsx pass through the same contract.

High-import shared facades (src/shared/lib/errorHandling/handleError.ts and src/integrations/supabase/client.ts) centralize access, but architectural ownership remains mostly coherent in this slice; the larger issue is boundary mixing in view-model and overlay-control contracts.

findings.summary: Core generation/shot domain types combine persisted data with UI-only overlays, weakening clear ownership boundaries.
evidence: src/domains/generation/types/generationViewRow.ts defines `GenerationRow = PersistedGenerationRow & GenerationViewModel`, where `GenerationViewModel` includes derived display fields and optimistic runtime field `isOptimistic`.
src/domains/generation/types/shotViewRow.ts defines `Shot = PersistedShotRow & ShotViewModel`, where `ShotViewModel` includes view/runtime counts and image arrays.

These merged types are consumed in runtime/UI flows (e.g., deletion hook workflows) as if they are canonical domain rows, increasing risk of accidental coupling between storage contracts and UI state.
findings.suggestion: Split canonical persisted entities from UI projections explicitly (for example, `PersistedGenerationRow`/`PersistedShotRow` as domain contracts, and separate `GenerationDisplayModel`/`ShotDisplayModel` in selector or view-model modules), then expose narrow mapping points where composition is required.

findings.summary: Open/close overlay ownership is distributed across shared components and page-level consumers, creating unclear responsibility for modal/tooltip lifecycle policy.
evidence: src/shared/components/ModalContainer.tsx defines its own `handleOpenChange` policy (`preventOutsideClose`) on top of externally provided `open/onOpenChange`.

src/shared/components/MediaTypeFilter.tsx and src/shared/components/MediaGalleryItem/components/InfoTooltip.tsx each accept optional overlay visibility props and internally normalize them again via `useOverlayVisibilityState`, creating repeated state-policy wrappers.

src/pages/Home/components/InstallInstructionsModal.tsx and src/shared/components/LoraSelectorModal/components/DescriptionModal.tsx pass the same open/onOpenChange contracts through shared modal infrastructure, but close semantics are partly local and partly container-driven.
findings.suggestion: Establish one architectural owner for overlay lifecycle policy (for example, container-level controllers/hooks) and keep shared presentational components stateless and contract-thin; standardize a single overlay contract entry point to eliminate repeated normalization wrappers.
